name: Loudoun County Public Schools
districtFact: One of the fastest-growing and wealthiest school districts on the East Coast, anchored by the Dulles Technology Corridor and Northern Virginia's data center industry.

about.title: About the District
about.content: Loudoun County Public Schools serves approximately 84,000 students in Loudoun County, Virginia — one of the wealthiest and fastest-growing counties in the United States, located in the outer Washington D.C. suburbs along the Dulles Technology Corridor.

planningTips: LCPS observes Diwali (Nov 9), Lunar New Year (Feb 5), Eid al-Fitr (Mar 9), and Yom Kippur (Sep 21) as student holidays. Families from other backgrounds should mark these as no-school days for childcare planning.
LCPS starts August 17 — one week earlier than Fairfax County and Prince William County (Aug 24). If coordinating schedules across NoVA districts, note the different start dates.
Spring Break (Mar 22–26) aligns with Fairfax and Prince William. Dulles Airport (IAD) is heavily booked during this window — book flights well in advance.
Loudoun's rapid growth means school zone boundaries change regularly. Verify your assigned school at lcps.org before signing a lease or purchasing a home in a new development area.

districtFaqs.q: Does Loudoun County observe Diwali and other cultural holidays as school holidays?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Loudoun County Public Schools is one of the few large school districts in the country to formally observe Diwali (November 9), Lunar New Year (February 5), Eid al-Fitr (March 9), and Yom Kippur (September 21) as no-school days. This reflects the county's significant Hindu, Asian American, Muslim, and Jewish communities. Families of all backgrounds should mark these as no-school days when planning childcare and appointments.

livingHere.intro: Loudoun County has transformed from Virginia's horse-and-wine country into one of the fastest-growing tech corridors in the United States, driven by the world's densest concentration of data centers and the proximity of Dulles International Airport. Western Loudoun retains its rural character with vineyards and equestrian estates, while eastern Loudoun around Ashburn and Sterling is suburban and rapidly expanding.

highlights.label: Data Center Alley
highlights.detail: The Route 7/28 corridor in eastern Loudoun hosts more data center square footage than any other place on earth — AWS, Microsoft Azure, and dozens of colocation providers employ thousands of tech workers who live in LCPS attendance zones.

highlights.label: Dulles Airport Is in Loudoun
highlights.detail: Washington Dulles International Airport (IAD) sits inside Loudoun County's eastern border, making international travel unusually accessible — but also generating significant aircraft noise in communities near the flight path.

highlights.label: Wine Country & Appalachian Trail
highlights.detail: Western Loudoun has 40+ licensed wineries and direct trail access to the Blue Ridge Mountains and Appalachian Trail — a weekend contrast to the suburban east that many families use for outdoor activities on school breaks.

highlights.label: Rapid School Zone Changes
highlights.detail: Loudoun opens new schools almost every year to keep pace with residential growth in Ashburn, Brambleton, and South Riding. Attendance boundaries shift frequently — verify your assigned school at lcps.org before signing any lease or purchase contract.

highlights.label: Historic Leesburg Downtown
highlights.detail: Leesburg, the county seat, has a preserved 18th-century downtown with independent shops and restaurants — a family-friendly destination distinct from the chain-heavy suburban corridors in eastern Loudoun.

highlights.label: Metro Silver Line Extension
highlights.detail: The Silver Line's Phase 2 extension opened stations in Loudoun at Dulles Airport and Ashburn, giving residents car-free access to D.C. and Tysons for the first time — reducing school-commute-era traffic pressure in the eastern corridor.
